attributes.stem:
An 80-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a two-day history of progressive confusion. He is accompanied by his son. He has a medical history of high alcohol use and hereditary haemochromatosis. He does not take any regular medications. His son is unsure when he last consumed alcohol.

On examination, he appears unwell with a pulse rate of 90/min, blood pressure 130/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 97% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. He is orientated to person but neither time nor place. He is jaundiced with large volume ascites and no abdominal tenderness. Examination of his hands reveals asterixis and no tremor. He is unsteady on his feet without truncal ataxia. His cranial nerve examination is normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the highest priority in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has presented with features of decompensated cirrhosis, including confusion, jaundice, ascites and asterixis. The presence of confusion and asterixis suggest hepatic encephalopathy, and therefore management of this must be the first priority. The aetiology of his cirrhosis is likely secondary to high alcohol use and hereditary haemochromatosis.

Hepatic encephalopathy is caused by the neurotoxic actions of ammonia, which is normally removed via the hepatobiliary route by a healthy liver. The build-up of ammonia leads to toxic effects, impaired brain energy metabolism, central pro-inflammatory mechanisms, and altered neurotransmission. Acute management of hepatic encephalopathy therefore involves ammonia-lowering strategies. Lactulose is a non-absorbable disaccharide that functions as a laxative. It reduces ammonia levels by acidification of the colon with resultant conversion of ammonia to ammonium, and by shifting the colonic flora from urease to non-urease producing bacterial species. It is the most effective management of hepatic encephalopathy, and therefore is recommended as first-line therapy.

Rifaximin is a non-absorbable antibiotic that is sometimes used as an adjunct therapy for hepatic encephalopathy. While it can be effective, particularly in preventing recurrence, it is not the first-line treatment for acute episodes and should not be prioritized over lactulose.

Oxazepam may be used in the management of alcohol withdrawal and is considered a relatively safe benzodiazepine in the setting of cirrhosis. While this patient has a history of high alcohol intake and alcohol withdrawal may certainly contribute to confusion, there are no other clear signs of withdrawal in the stem such as tremor or diaphoresis and this is therefore a less immediate treatment priority. Alcohol withdrawal should nevertheless be carefully monitored for and managed.

Thiamine replacement therapy is used to treat patients presenting with Wernicke encephalopathy, a complication of chronic alcohol use. While this patient does have confusion, his lack of gait ataxia and ophthalmoplegia make a diagnosis of Wernicke encephalopathy less likely, and therefore thiamine replacement is not a priority treatment for this patient's current presentation.

Therapeutic paracentesis may be considered for symptomatic management of the patient's ascites, however this is not the highest treatment priority in this setting. Although there are no overt clinical features of spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, an ascitic fluid sample should be obtained as part of the workup to determine the cause of this patient's hepatic encephalopathy. Other common precipitants include gastrointestinal bleeding, constipation and electrolyte derangements.
learningPoints: The priority of treatment in acute hepatic encephalopathy is early lactulose administration.
Lactulose reduces ammonia levels by acidifying the colon and altering colonic flora.
references: Butterworth R. Hepatic encephalopathy in alcoholic cirrhosis. Handb Clin Neurol. 2014;125:589-602. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25307598/t

Cirrhosis in over 16s: assessment and management. In: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) Clinical guideline NG50 [online]. 2016; cited 2022 Jan 4. Available at: https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng50
Vasan S, Kumar A. Wernicke Encephalopathy. In: Statpearls [online]. 2021; cited 2022 Jan 4. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470344/
